Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
v
Preface
The Art and Design Teaching Module for Form 1 has been developed in response to the 
introduction of the Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC), a transformative initiative aimed at 
enhancing education quality and relevance.
This teaching module is designed to support teachers in effectively delivering Art and Design 
lessons under the new curriculum. Its primary objectives are to; bridge the gap created by the 
absence of approved textbooks and equip teachers with practical skills and assessments tailored 
to learners’ needs and levels
As the Ministry of Education, we acknowledge the challenges associated with transitioning to a 
new curriculum. Consequently, this module reflects our commitment to providing teachers with 
structured guidance, suggested activities, and formative and summative assessments aligned 
with the CBC’s objectives.
While this module serves as a foundational resource, we encourage teachers to supplement it 
with their own research and innovations to address potential gaps during implementation. The 
suggested activities and assessments are designed to foster practical and critical thinking skills 
in learners, ensuring relevance and adaptability across diverse school contexts.
Teachers are encouraged to tailor these suggestions to their unique classroom dynamics and 
explore alternative approaches when necessary. We hope this module will empower teachers 
to navigate the new Competence-Based Curriculum with confidence, inspiring and equipping 
learners with essential competencies, knowledge, skills and values.
Kelvin Mambwe  (PhD)
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

Art and Design
Teaching Module
vi
Acknowledgements
The Art and Design Teaching Module for Form 1 is the culmination of a collaborative and 
inclusive development process. The Ministry of Education wishes to express appreciation to 
all those who participated in the processes of developing the first ever Module for Form 1 and 
their valuable contributions. Their invaluable input and expertise have enriched the content and 
quality of this teaching module.
I also acknowledge the dedication and hard work of the staff at the Curriculum Development 
Centre, whose tireless efforts ensured the successful completion of the module.
Charles Ndakala (Dr.)
Director - Curriculum Development
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
vii
Introduction
Welcome to this comprehensive module on Art and Design. This module is designed to introduce 
form 1 Educators to the fundamental principles and practices of art and design, encompassing 
various categories, branches and styles. Learners will explore the significance of art awareness 
and appreciation, covering visual, performing, and literary arts.
Through this module, educators will gain a deeper understanding of:
 
 History of Zambian Art
 
 Graphic Design
 
 Pattern and Design
 
 Drawing and Painting from imaginative
 
 Drawing from observation
 
 Drawing from still Life
 
 Figure Drawing
By the end of this module, form 1 educators will be equipped with the knowledge and skills to 
promote art awareness and appreciation and to develop their learners’ creative, technical, and 
critical thinking skills.

Art and Design
Teaching Module
viii
Icons used in the module
Summary                          	
Terms/Vocabulary   
     
 Assessment                 	
	
Tips          
                            
  Activity

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
1
1.2 HISTORY OF ZAMBIAN ART
SUB-TOPIC 1.2.3 POST-INDEPENDENCE CONTEMPORARY ART
Introduction
In this subtopic, learners will study Post Independence Contemporary Art in Zambia which 
reflects the country’s cultural heritage and modern influences. Characterized by a blend of 
traditional and modern styles, Zambian artists address local themes like poverty, inequality, and 
environmental concerns. 
Some of the notable artists (Agness Buya Yombwe, Flinto Chandia, Caleb Chisha…) and 
galleries (Moto Moto Museum, Lechwe Trust Art Gallery, Wayi Wayi Art Gallery…) promote 
intergenerational conversations and collaborations. Despite the various challenges faced, the art 
scene continues to grow, driven by individual artists and supporters. Overall, Post-Independence 
Contemporary Art in Zambia showcases the country’s rich cultural identity and its ongoing 
evolution.
Specific Competence
1.2.3.1 Explain Post-Independence Contemporary Art
Learning Activities
Learning Environment set up: 
 
 artificial (Artificial Intelligence- AI, smart phone…) 
 
 in-door (classroom…)  
 
 out-door (school premises…)
 
 natural (community…)
 
 technological (digital and audio…)
 Suggested Learning & Teaching Materials:
(i)	
 Charts and diagrams on Post-independence Contemporary Art  
(ii)	
Videos/documentaries about Post-Independence Contemporary Art
(iii)	
Art and Design textbooks  
Activity 1:  Explaining Post-Independence Contemporary Art
Discussion
Task 1: Research on the works of notable Zambian Contemporary Artists
 Learners to research on notable Zambian Post-Independence Contemporary Artists
Discussion and Pair Work
Discussion
Task 2: In pairs, analyse specific art pieces that reflect the characteristics of Post-Independence 
Contemporary Art in Zambia

Art and Design
Teaching Module
2
Tip
Write learners answers on the board as they present and outline researched characteristics of 
Zambian Post-Independence Contemporary Art 
Expected standard: Post-independence Contemporary Art explained correctly
Assessment
 
 This will involve evaluating the significance, impact and contributions of the country’s cultural 
heritage, through Post-Independence Contemporary Art
Summary
This topic looked at Post-Independence Contemporary Art in Zambia and how it has 
changed over the years. The learners explored the Post Contemporary art in Zambia 
which has evolved significantly, reflecting the countries cultural heritage and modern 
influences.
SUB-TOPIC 1.2.4 TRADITIONAL DECORATIONS
Introduction
Traditional decorations in Zambia are a vibrant reflection of the country’s rich cultural heritage 
and diversity. These decorations feature a range of artistic expressions, including intricately 
woven basketry, carved wood items, colourful textiles, and beautiful beadwork. Each of these 
decorative form carries significant cultural meanings, often symbolizing stories, traditions, and 
community values.
From the intricate designs of basketry to the symbolic carvings of woodwork and the vibrant 
patterns of Textiles, traditional decorations in Zambia showcase the country’s diverse cultural 
practices. These decorations not only add beauty to homes and public spaces, but also play a vital 
role in ceremonies, celebrations, and community life, highlighting the importance of preserving 
Zambia’s cultural heritage.
Specific Competence: 
1.2.4.1 Make traditional decorations
Learning Activities
Learning Environment set up: 
 
 in-door (classroom…)  
 
 out-door (school premises)
 
 natural (immediate environment)
 
 artificial (artificial Intelligence- AI…) 
 
 technological (digital and audio)

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
3
 Suggested Learning & Teaching Materials:
(i)	
 Charts and diagrams on decorations 
(ii)	
Video Tutorials on making decorations
(iii)	
Art and Design textbooks  
Activity 1:  Making traditional decorations (Hand prints, jewellery…)
Discussion /Research
Task 1: Research the different Traditional Decorations (Hand prints, jewellery…) found in Zambia.
Tip
Take extra caution to distinguish traditional decorations and where they are locally 
found
Demonstration and Project work
Task 2: Make traditional decorations (Hand prints, jewellery…)
Tip
Ensure that the traditional decorations (Hand prints, jewellery…) made by the learners 
are appropriately crafted, stating their respective functional uses. 
Expected standard: Traditional decorations made skilfully.
Assessment
 
 This will involve evaluating the process, product and cultural significance in 
craftsmanship. Various materials of traditional decorations in Zambia are evaluated by 
using different criteria, the making, appreciation of cultural significance and artistic 
value of these creations.
Summary
This topic looked at the making of traditional decorations (Hand prints, jewellery…) 
and how technical skills are applied, the cultural significance of the products, creativity 
and originality applied as well as presentation and display.
SUB-TOPIC 1.2.5 TRADITIONAL ARTIFACTS
Introduction
In this subtopic, Traditional artefacts in Zambia are physical objects that hold significant 
cultural, historical, and spiritual value to the country’s diverse communities. These artefacts can 
include masks, sculptures, Textiles and jewellery. These artefacts have distinct Significance in 
cultural identity, Spiritual significance and historical importance. These help to promote Cultural 
Preservation, Education and awareness.

Art and Design
Teaching Module
4
Traditional artefacts in Zambia are a vital part of the country’s cultural heritage, and their 
preservation and promotion are essential for maintaining the country’s rich cultural identity.
Specific Competence: 
1.2.5.1 Create traditional Artefacts.
Learning Activities
Learning Environment set up:
 
 in-door (classroom…)  
 
 out-door (school premises)
 
 natural (immediate environment)
 
 artificial (Online platforms…) 
 
 technological (digital and audio)
 Suggested Learning & Teaching Materials:
(i)	
Charts and diagrams on traditional Artefacts  
(ii)	
Video Tutorials on traditional Artefacts
(iii)	
Art and Design textbooks  
Activity 1:  Creating traditional Artefacts (Basketry, weaving, pottery, beadwork…)
Research
Task 1: Research on the different traditional Artefacts (Basketry, weaving, pottery, bead­
work…)) found in Zambia.
Tip
Take extra caution to distinguish among the Traditional Artefacts (Basketry, weaving, pottery, 
beadwork…) and where they are found locally.
Demonstration and Project work
Task 2: Create traditional Artefacts (Basketry, weaving, pottery, beadwork…)
Expected standard: Traditional Artefacts created skilfully.
Assessment
This will involve evaluating the process, product and cultural significance in craftsmanship 
and use of material in the creation of traditional artefacts. 
Summary
This topic looked at the Creating of traditional Artefacts (Basketry, weaving, pottery, beadwork…) 
and how technical skills are applied, the cultural significance of the products, creativity and 
originality applied as well as presentation and display.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
5
TOPIC 1.5  GRAPHIC DESIGN  
SUB-TOPIC 1.5.1  POSTER MAKING
Introduction
In this subtopic, learners will explore the art of Poster making, focusing on how to convey 
important messages related to cross-cutting issues such as conservation and social activities. 
Through creative design, learners will learn to express ideas visually.
 Specific Competence
1.5.1.1 Design various posters on crosscutting issues to convey messages.
Learning Activities
1.	 Identifying examples of posters
2.	 Designing various posters on crosscutting issues to convey messages (conservations, 
social activities)
Learning Environment set-up: In-door / out-door (natural, artificial and technological)
 
 Classroom with access to design materials (paper, markers, computers)
 
 Access to examples of posters (physical or digital)
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials: 
i.	
Markers
ii.	
Coloured pencils
iii.	
Pencils/Pens/brushes
iv.	
Paper/ card paper
v.	
Paints 
Activity 1:  Identifying examples of posters
Discussion
Task 1:
Identify and analyse posters that effectively communicate messages on cross-cutting issues 
(Gender Based Violence, Betting, Climate change…) 
Tip
 Focus on what makes these posters effective. Consider the use of colour, imagery and text.
Activity 2: Designing various posters on crosscutting issues to convey messages on cross-cutting 
issues (conservations, social activities…)

Art and Design
Teaching Module
6
Demonstration / Project Work
Task 1:
Design three types of posters based on cross-cutting issues (informative, advertising, 
educative…)
Tip
1.	 Learners to apply upper and lower case typography in the posters designed
2.	 Designed posters should have clear interpretations of given themes (intended 
communication)
3.	 Learners to identify and describe the three types of posters (informative, advertising, 
educative…) respectively
Critique
Task 2: Display and critique designed posters
Tip
Ensure posters have a clear message, appropriate use of colours for the intended target 
audience.
Expected Standard
Various posters on crosscutting issues to convey messages designed creatively 
Assessment
 
z
Clarity of message
 
z
Creativity and originality
 
z
Effective use of colours and design elements
 
z
Overall aesthetic appeal.
Summary
This topic emphasised the importance of Graphic Design in communicating vital mes­
sages through posters. Learners gained practical skills in poster creation while exploring 
significant social and environmental issues.
SUB-TOPIC 1.5.2 COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN (CAD)
Introduction:
Computer Aided Design (CAD) is a powerful tool used by designers to create precise drawings 
and models. This topic will introduce learners to the fundamentals of CAD and its application 
in designing various types of (e-cards, business, greeting cards, Architectural Designs, Product 
Designs, Mechanical Designs…)

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
7
Specific Competence:
1.5.2.1 Design various types of cards using a computer
Learning Activities
Learning Environment Set-up:
i.	
Indoor: Computer lab with CAD software installed.
ii.	
Outdoor: Access to natural light for inspiration.
iii.	
Technological: Use of projectors for demonstrations and presentations.
Suggested Learning & Teaching Materials:
	
 Computers
	
 Projector and screen
	
 Sample card designs (printed and digital)
	
 Art supplies for brainstorming sessions
Activity 1: Defining Computer Aided Design
Discussion
Task 1: Define and discuss Computer Aided Design (CAD)
Group Work
Activity 2: Designing various types of cards using a computer (E-cards, animated cards. Digital 
invitation, Christmas, anniversary, birthday, graduation, Easter, Kitchen party…)
Exploration/ Research
Task 1:  Make designs using a computer
Activity 3: Relating careers to computer aided designing (Architectural Designer, Product 
Designer, Mechanical Designer…)
Research Project: (learners explore different careers that utilize CAD)
Task 1: Research and present on a career related to CAD (Architectural Designer, Product 
Designer…).
Tip
Invite a guest speaker (Computer Studies Teacher/ Expert) to share their experiences 
and the importance of CAD in their work.
Expected standard: Various cards designed using a computer accurately.

Art and Design
Teaching Module
8
Assessment
 
z
Evaluate the designs based on creativity, adherence to design principles, and technical 
execution in CAD.
 
z
Group presentations on career research will be assessed for content and presentation 
skills.
Summary
This topic provides foundational knowledge and practical skills in Computer Aided 
Design, preparing learners for further studies or careers in graphic design and related 
fields.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
9
TOPIC 1.6 PATTERN AND DESIGN 
Introduction
Patterns and Designs 
The topic discusses patterns and designs around the environment, shaping our emotions, 
behaviours and interactions. Patterns influence our experiences through various forms 
(architecture, fashion…).
This topic, will explore the world of patterns, designs, covering their forms, applications and 
their significance. By doing so, patterns and designs will be appreciated in relation to how they 
impact lives around the world. The sub topics will further explore the basics of pattern and 
design, looking at different types and how they are used in art (buildings, clothes…) 
General Competences: Creative and innovation, analytical thinking, collaboration, problem 
solving skills.
Key Terms /Words/Vocabulary
	

Motif: repeated element or design unit used in a pattern or composition. It can be a 
shape, symbol, or image that is repeated to create a larger design.
	

Repetition: repeated use of a motif or element in a design or pattern. It can create 
rhythm, unity, and emphasis.
	

 Rhythm: the creation of a visual tempo or beat through the repetition of elements, 
such as motifs or colours. It can guide the viewer’s eye through the composition.
	

 Symmetry: the quality of being balanced and harmonious in terms of shape, size, 
and position. It occurs when one half of an object or design is a mirror image of the 
other half.
	

Asymmetry:  occurs when an object or design does not have a mirror image or 
balance between its parts. Asymmetry can create visual interest and dynamic com­
position.
SUB-TOPIC 1.6.1 PATTERNS 
Introduction
In this topic, the focus is aligned with Pattern design which is the creation of repeating designs 
or motifs, often used to add visual interest and beauty to various surfaces, objects and materials. 
Pattern design involves the arrangement of shapes, colours and textures in a repetitive and 
harmonious way. 
Pattern also uses the art elements in planned or random repetition to enhance surfaces or paintings 
or sculptures. Patterns often occur in nature, and artists use similar repeated motifs to create 
pattern in their work. Pattern increases visual excitement by enriching surface interest. 
Specific Competence: 
1.6.1.1 Make patterns

Art and Design
Teaching Module
10
Learning Activities
 
z
Learning Environment set up: 
	
 in-door (classroom…)  
	
 out-door (school premises) 
	
 natural (natural motifs- flowers, leaves, butterflies…)
	
 artificial (textured shoe sole, textured wall, pictures…) 
	
 technological (digital/ video watching)
 Suggested Learning & Teaching Materials:
(i)	
Charts and diagrams on patterns 
(ii)	
Videos/documentaries about patterns 
(iii)	
Printed motifs/ printed fabric (Chitenge…)
(iv)	
Art and Design textbooks  
(v)	
Matching Artworks of patterns
Activity 1:  Identifying examples of patterns (Artificial and natural…) 
Demonstration/ Research/ Discussion
TASK 1: Create examples of artificial and natural patterns using various materials (paper, 
paint, fabric…)
Tip
 Learners to research and make illustrations on artificial and natural patterns.
Task 2: Identify and label examples of artificial and natural patterns in a given set of images. 
Artificial patterns (stripes and polka dots in fabric, wall paper, ceramics, Geometric shapes in 
Architecture design) Natural patterns (leaves and flowers for shapes, colours, arrangements. 
Rocks for textures and formation. 
Tip
Display learner’s works in order to demonstrate knowledge of artificial and natural 
patterns. 
Activity 2: Identifying motif types
Discussion and Pair work
Task 1: In pairs, sort the different types of identified Motifs (Geometric, Organic, Abstract…)
Tip
 Write learners answers on the chalk board as they present and outline the correct types 
of motifs with learners after presentations.	
Activity 3: Exploring with various motif designs
Demonstration/Exploration 
TASK 1: Explore various motif designs by creating new designs using various materials 
(paper, paint, fabric…)

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
11
Tip
1.  Encourage learners to create variation of an existing motif design, exploring different 
colours, shapes and pattern. 
2. Learners can combine two or more motif designs to create new and unique designs.
Activity 4:  Making patterns (Random, regular) Block (potato printing…), stencil, linocut and 
intaglio
Discussion
Task 1: Discuss the materials used for each technique (potato for potato printing, stencils 
for various shapes and designs, linoleum blocks for linocut printing, intaglio plates for 
intaglio printing, ink and paint for various colours, paper and fabric for various textures 
and colours…)
Exploration
Task 2: Make patterns (Random, regular) using various techniques, (potato printing, 
stencil printing, linocut printing, intaglio printing…)
Tip
1. Encourage learners to incorporate pattern inspirations into their own designs
2. Guide learners to experiment with different materials techniques and patterns
Expected standard: Patterns made creatively
Assessment
	
 Observation of learners during pattern making activities
	
 Review of learners’ art works for composition, texture and colour.
Summary
 This topic looked at a variety of creative expressions of Pattern designs and types of 
patterns.

Art and Design
Teaching Module
12
TOPIC: 1.7 DRAWING AND PAINTING FROM IMAGINATION
Introduction: 
This topic will help learners unlock the power of their imagination, it will give them freedom to 
explore their thoughts, emotions, experiences and express them in a visual language. Drawing 
and painting from imagination is a creative process that allows learners to tap into their inner 
world and bring forth unique and innovative ideas. 
It involves generating art works from ones’ own imagination. It will also allow them cultivate 
their creativity and originality, express their emotions and inner world and create innovative art 
works.  By drawing and painting from imagination, learners will develop their critical thinking 
and problem solving skills.  
General Competences: 
Creative and Innovation, Analytical thinking, Collaboration, Communication.
Key Terms /Words/Vocabulary
 
 Creativity (The ability to generate new and original ideas.)
 
 Imagination (The ability to form mental images or concepts.)
 
 Abstraction (the process of simplifying forms into essential elements)
 
 Expressionism (Art movement that emphasizes expressing emotions and inner 
experiences.) 
SUB-TOPIC 1.7.1 IMAGINATIVE COMPOSITIONS
Introduction
Imaginative Composition is the creative process of arranging visual elements and principles to 
convey visual representations, ideas, emotions or stories. It involves imagination and artistic 
skills to create meaningful images. The learners will be exposed to Imaginative composition as 
a key aspect of drawing and painting. It involves arranging visual elements to evoke emotions, 
convey ideas and engage the viewer’s imagination in order to attend to situations outside real-life 
(fiction, abstract, non-realistic narrations…)
 Specific Competence:
1.7.1.1 Compose pictures based on a given theme
Learning Activities
Learning Environment set up: 
 
 in-door (classroom set-up…)
 
 out-door (at the market, school premises, outside the classroom…)
 
 natural (interacting with natural /organic shapes…)
 
 artificial (graphic software, photo montage…)
 
 technological (graphics, Computer Aided, Artificial intelligence- AI…)

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
13
Suggested Learning & Teaching Materials:
i.	
Reference books
ii.	
Online materials (pictures, videos…)
iii.	
Samples of already made artworks
iv.	
Paper
v.	
Paint, pencils, markers, pens 
Learning Activities
Activity 1:  Composing pictures from given themes (my family, market place, hunting…)
Brainstorming and Discussion
Task 1: Discuss Imaginative composition 
Tip
1.	 Learners are introduced to concepts of Imaginative composition and its importance 
to Art.
2.	 Display a video/ show examples of imaginative compositions from various artists and 
styles.
Task 2: Select one of the given themes that resonate appropriately with guided Imaginative 
composition titles (My family, market place, hunting...)
Tip
1.	 Give learners a list of themes to choose from in order to compose imaginative pictures.
2.	 Encourage learners to take a few minutes to brainstorm ideas related to their chosen 
theme. They can think about their personal experiences, memories or emotions 
associated with the theme.
Activity 2: Interpreting ideas and Crosscutting issues (environmental education, GBV, National 
values and principles…)
Demonstration
Task 1: Learners create art works using recycled materials, depicting environmental issues 
(climate change, conservation…)
Tip
Engage learners in a debate assigning them with different perspectives on an 
environmental issue for better interpretation of ideas and crosscutting issues.
Activity 3: Drawing and Painting (Landscape, Village Scenery, Townscape…)
Field Trip and Discussion
Task 1: Go on a field trip to a nearby park or village scenery /town scape to observe and

Art and Design
Teaching Module
14
sketch (landscapes, village scenery, town scape…)
Task 2: Learners draw and paint imaginative pictures based on the ideas got from the tour.
Tip
Organise a field trip or virtual tour for learners to evoke / insight imaginative experi­
ences.
 Expected Standard: Pictures based on a given theme composed creatively
Summary
In this topic, we looked at how drawing and painting from imaginations unlock the 
power of imagination. It gave learners the freedom to explore their thoughts, emotions, 
experiences and expressed them in a visual language. Learners also cultivated their 
creativity and originality, expressed their emotions as well as their inner world and 
created innovative art works.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
15
TOPIC 1.8 DRAWING AND PAINTING FROM OBSERVATION
Introduction: 
Drawing and painting from observation is a fundamental skill for artists, allowing them to 
accurately capture the world around them.
This topic explores the techniques and principles of drawing and painting from observation, 
helping artists refine their skills and create more realistic and engaging works. By observing and 
rendering subjects directly, artists develop their ability to see and represent the finer details of 
light, form, texture, and colour.
By directly observing their subjects, artists can (develop their observational skills and attention to 
detail, improve their rendering techniques and artistic expression, create authentic and realistic 
representations of the world…)
General competence(s): 
Analytical Thinking, Communication, Creativity and Innovation, Collaboration, Critical 
Thinking
Key Terms /Words/Vocabulary
Observation:	the act of carefully studying and noting the details of a subject
Rendering: 	 creating a detailed and accurate representation of a subject
Proportion: 	 the relationship between the size and scale of different parts of a subject
Perspective: 	 creating the illusion of three-dimensional space on a two-dimensional 
surface
Value: 	
the lightness or darkness of a colour or object
Chiaroscuro:	use of strong contrasts between light and dark to create a sense of volume 
and depth 
Colour theory: the study of how colours interact and affect each other
SUB-TOPIC: 1.8.1 DRAWING OR PAINTING FROM NATURAL     
OR MAN–MADE OBJECTS 
Introduction:
Drawing and painting from observation enhances learner’s ability to capture the world around 
them accurately. This topic focuses on observing and representing both Natural and Man-made 
objects through various drawing techniques.
Specific Competence:
1.8.1.1 Draw and or paint pictures from observation
Learning Activities
Learning Environment Setup:
	
 Indoor (Art room with good lighting, easels…)

Art and Design
Teaching Module
16
	
 Outdoor (Access to gardens, parks, or urban settings for live observation…)
	
 Technological (Use of tablets for digital drawing/painting applications…)
Suggested Learning & Teaching Materials:
i.	
Drawing/ Painting (bond paper, sugar paper, black paper…) and sketchbooks
ii.	
Pencils, charcoal, paints (watercolour, acrylic…)
iii.	
Easels (drawing/ painting stand) and drawing boards
iv.	
Reference images of objects (if needed)
Activity 1: Drawing and/or Painting from Observation
Demonstration/ Guided Practice
Task 1: Choose a natural object (tree, flower, fruit…) and create a detailed drawing or painting 
from observation.
Tip
Encourage learners to focus on light effect on natural objects (shadow, texture …) to 
enhance realism in their work.
Demonstration/ Guided Practice
Task 2: Select a Man-made object (a building, a piece of furniture…) and create a 
complete drawing or painting that highlights its features.
Tip
 |
1. Encourage learners to focus on light effect on Man-made objects (shadow, texture 
…) to enhance realism in their work
2. Emphasise on the need to capture the foreground, mid-ground and background as 
part of the compositions
Expected Standard:  Pictures from observation drawn and or painted skillfully
Assessment
 
 Evaluate leaners’ artworks based on accuracy, creativity, and the application of 
techniques taught and skills acquired.
 
 Provide constructive feedback during peer reviews.
Summary
This topic exposed learners to acquire skills in drawing from observation and represent 
their surroundings through drawings and paintings, fostering artistic skills and 
appreciation of the visual world of  Natural and Man-made objects.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
17
TOPIC 1.9 DRAWING AND PAINTING FROM STILL LIFE
Introduction
In this topic, the learners will acquire skills such as, creativity, observation and analytical 
thinking. Still life drawing and painting is an artistic style that captures the aesthetics and essence 
of static objects, often arranged in deliberate compositions. From everyday household items like 
fruit and books to symbolic or luxurious objects, artists use light, colour, texture, proportion and 
composition to convey meaning and emotion. 
By exploring the relationships between objects, artists create visually captivating works that 
reveal the extraordinary in the ordinary, often touching on themes like mortality, aesthetics, 
and the passage of time. Through Still Life art, artists can experiment with technique, challenge 
perceptions and invite viewers to slow down and appreciate the details of the everyday world.
Key Terms /Words/Vocabulary
Composition: Arrangement of objects in a still life setup.
Proportion: 	 Relationship between the size of objects in the setup.
Value: Lightness or darkness of objects, used to create contrast.
Colour / Hue: is an important art element that defines the reflection of light on an object.
Still life arrangement / Drawing motif: Objects arranged in a specific way to create a 
composition (a recurring theme element or pattern used in a composition to create unity, 
emphasis or rhythm adding depth and visual interest in an art work…)
SUB-TOPIC 1.9.1 STILL LIFE DRAWING AND OR PAINTING
Introduction 
Still life drawing and painting offers numerous benefits that can enhance skills, creativity and 
overall artistic growth. Fundamental skills like; Observation, form rendering and color theory 
are developed.
Specific Competence: 
1.9.1.1 Compose pictures from Still life
Learning Activities
Learning Environment Set-up: 
 
 in-door (art room, studio…)
 
 out-door (a stack of arranged rocks, at the scrap metal junk yard, part of an engine...)
 
 technological (mechanical approach, digital platforms…)
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials: 
i.	
Paint Brushes
ii.	
Pencils: Graphite, charcoal, or coloured pencils used for drawing.

18
iii.

Activity 1: Composing pictures from Still life
Discussion, Demonstration and Individual work
Task 1. Learners should sketch and draw arranged house-hold objects (pot, cup, dish, sugar 
bowl…).
Tip
1.	 Encourage learners to observe the set-up carefully and pay attention to: 
 
 Shape, texture and form
 
 Value (light and dark)
 
 Balance and proportion
2.	 Demonstrate step by step how to capture a given Still life arrangement, display 
already made teaching aids or invite an expert. Leaners can be shown online drawing/
painting tutorials.  
Expected standard: Pictures from still-life composed appropriately.
Assessment
 
 Evaluate Still life drawings based on shape, texture, form, value (light and dark), 
balance and proportion.
 
 Peer- Self assessment: task leaners to assess and provide feedback on each other’s 
works, promoting critical thinking and constructive criticism.
Summary
In this sub-topic, different pictures were composed from Still-life drawing and or 
painting. The key techniques and principles acquired and applied were, observation, 
rendering, composition, colour theory, lighting and shading.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
19
TOPIC 1.10 FIGURE DRAWING
Introduction
The topic will discuss the fundamental skills like proportion, pose, gesture and line art. Stick-
Figure introduces the learners to basics on the use of line and shape in order to create simple 
imagery.
Stick Figure (human) Drawing is the art of creating simple animate features using lines in 
various shapes to represent the torso and the limbs which leads to more complex designs such as 
animations, infographics, signage and other defined visual media.
General Competences: 
Analytical Thinking, Collaboration, Communication, Creativity and Innovation, Critical 
Thinking
Key Terms /Words/Vocabulary
Medium: The material or technique used to create a work of art, such as painting, 
drawing, sculpture, or digital art.
Line: A continuous mark made on a surface by a drawing tool, used to define shape, 
suggest movement, or create texture.
Shape: A self-contained area with height and width, often used to create composition, 
balance, and harmony in a work of art.
Balance: The arrangement of visual elements to create a sense of stability and equilibrium, 
often achieved through symmetry, asymmetry, or radial composition.
SUB-TOPIC: 1.10.1. STICK FIGURE DRAWING
Introduction
Stick Figure Drawing is a creative process that involves generating visual representations on a 
two-dimensional surface, such as paper, canvas, or digital screens. This art form utilizes var­
ious techniques, including drawing, painting, and digital illustration, to produce images that 
convey emotions, ideas, and messages.
Specific Competence: 
1.10.1.1 Compose Stick-Figures
Learning Activities
Learning Environment set-up: 
 
 In-door: (classroom, studio, computer lab…)
 
 Technological (mechanical approach, digital platforms…)
Suggested Learning & Teaching Materials:
(i)	 Pencil
(ii)	Paper/ wall surfaces
(iii)	Eraser

Art and Design
Teaching Module
20
Activity 1: Defining Stick Figure Drawing
Discussion
Task 1: Learners define and discuss Stick Figure Drawing
Tip
Give the learners a brief account about the value of Stick Figure Drawing as a prelude 
to major designing.
Demonstration
Activity 2: Composing Stick-figures (Persons in action…)
Discussion and demonstration
TASK 1: Design a Stick-figure of Persons in action such as: 
1.	 Running
2.	 Walking 
3.	 Swimming
4.	 Cooking 
5.	 Bathing 
Tip
1.	 Emphasize on the concept of visual storyline in a short creation 
Note: Stick Figure Drawings offer several advantages such as;
i.	
Minimal materials
ii.	
Minimal skill required
iii.	
Easily and clearly conveys actions, poses and basic concepts
iv.	
Creates visual notes for easy recall	
Expected standard: Stick-figures composed creatively
Assessment
	
 Make an assessment to ensure that learners develop a deeper comprehension and 
value of Stick Figure Drawing.
	
 Prepare the tasks and allocate time for practical and oral assessments based on Stick 
Figure Drawings.
Summary
This topic, looked at composing Stick Figure Drawings in action, simplicity and clarity 
over detailed realism.
The ultimate expectation in this topic was to help learners develop fundamental drawing 
skills, practice gesture drawing and create simple, yet expressive and proportionate 
figures.

LESSON EVALUATION: 
Having taught the lesson, 65 out of the 75 learners present, managed to achieve the expected 
lesson standards and competences. 10 out of the 75 learners did not achieve the expected lesson 
competences and standards, this was due to their inability to achieve first stages of sketching and 
drawing the objects in good time. The learners were then given remedial time during prep time 
and given carry-home work.
The teacher was able to ensure collaboration was upheld throughout the lesson and gave individ­
ual learner attention at sketching and drawing stages.
Overall, the lesson was successfully attained at about 86% rate.
